coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-derived ringent hollow carbon microspheres towards high-efficient microwave absorption
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Yinger
Zheng, Jinfei
Luo, Heng
Rehman, Hira
Zou, Guoqiang
Tong, Jinchao
Hou, Hongshuai
Ji, Xiaobo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials have steadily grown into a class of composite absorbers with remarkable properties due to their unique structure, low density, high specific surface area, strong electrical conductivity, and substantial dielectric losses. In this paper, a facile strategy with low-cost, high-efficiency, and scalable strategy for carbon dots-derived ringent hollow carbon (RHC) for superior microwave absorption is proposed. Controllable structures ranging from amorphous carbon to ringent hollow carbon are well performed through simple heat-treated temperature. In addition, an optimal absorption value of −67 dB and a superior absorption band width covering the C, X, and Ku bands, as well as a relative compression ratio of 56.7 percent in the case of perpendicular incidence, have been achieved. Further, finite element simulations have demonstrated that the unique structure of partially ringent hollow carbon is considered as an effective way to lower normal reflected wave and promote energy attenuation. New applications of carbon dots-derived RHC as superior microwave absorbers are opening up, and the scalable strategy may pave a solid foundation for rapid applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ringent hollow carbon
Controllable structure
RCS reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM, TEM, and HRTEM images of RHC-400 (a1–a4), RHC-600 (b1–b4), RHC-700 (c1–c4) and RHC-800 (d1–d4).

Structure characterization. XRD patterns (a), pore size distribution (b–c), nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms (d), XPS survey spectra (e), the C 1s spectra of RHC-400 (f), RHC-600 (g), RHC-700 (h) and RHC-800 (i).

Microwave electromagnetic spectrum: RHC-400 (a), RHC-600 (b), RHC-700 (c) and RHC-800 (d). 3D plots of reflection loss curves versus thickness: RHC-400 (e), RHC-600 (f), RHC-700 (g) and RHC-800 (h). Microwave impedance versus thickness: RHC-400 (i), RHC-600 (j), RHC-700 (k) and RHC-800 (l).

3D plot (a) and Polar diagram of radar cross section (RCS) curves of RHC-700 enabled microwave absorbing coating (marked as red) as well as metal plane (marked as blue) (b). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Spatial distribution of volume loss of RHCs: RHC-400 (a), RHC-600 (b), RHC-700 (c) and RHC-800 (d). The a1 shows the SEM photo, a2 and a3 plot 3D view and cross section view of volume loss, a4 plots the scattering diagram of vector E field, and so on in a similar fashion.
Volume loss density and reference value of reflection coefficient evolution with temperature increasing. (The reflection coefficient of S-400 is referred to as normalized value 1.)

Scattering field distribution versus opening direction of RHCs: simulation of model (a), cloud map distribution at top view (b), scattering field intensity (c) and cloud map distribution at cross-section view (d).
The formation mechanism of ringent hollow carbons.
Comparison of the microwave absorption performance of various carbon microspheres.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-derived ringent hollow carbon microspheres towards high-efficient microwave absorption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Electronic Information, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, School of Electronic Information, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Nanyang Avenue, 639798, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Nanyang Avenue, 639798, Singapore

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yinger Xiang and Jinfei Zheng contribute equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ringent hollow carbons synthesised by CDs through controllable reaction temperatures show great absorbing values and absorbing bands as well as relative compression ratios of radar cross section. Finite element simulation demonstrats that unique structure of ringent hollow carbons can lower normal reflected wave and promote energy attenuation which provides a new direction for the structural design of absorbers.

Carbon nanomaterials have steadily grown into a class of composite absorbers with remarkable properties due to their unique structure, low density, high specific surface area, strong electrical conductivity, and substantial dielectric losses. In this paper, a facile strategy with low-cost, high-efficiency, and scalable strategy for carbon dots-derived ringent hollow carbon (RHC) for superior microwave absorption is proposed. Controllable structures ranging from amorphous carbon to ringent hollow carbon are well performed through simple heat-treated temperature. In addition, an optimal absorption value of −67 dB and a superior absorption band width covering the C, X, and Ku bands, as well as a relative compression ratio of 56.7 percent in the case of perpendicular incidence, have been achieved. Further, finite element simulations have demonstrated that the unique structure of partially ringent hollow carbon is considered as an effective way to lower normal reflected wave and promote energy attenuation. New applications of carbon dots-derived RHC as superior microwave absorbers are opening up, and the scalable strategy may pave a solid foundation for rapid applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The proliferation of electronic devices in various industries has led to a growing concern over the escalation of electromagnetic (EM) radiation and interference, particularly concerning high-frequency electron devices [1–5]. Absorbing materials can effectively reduce the harm of electromagnetic radiation by absorbing or shielding electromagnetic wave energy [6–12]. Until now, various EM absorbing/shielding materials with different compositions and microstructures have been developed and paved the way for EMC application. The fundamental process in absorbing matter is that the charge carriers reciprocate under the action of the applied electromagnetic field, forming a microscopic instantaneous current, which eventually converts the electromagnetic wave energy into heat through the thermal effect of the current [13,14].

Carbon nanomaterials, particularly carbon nanosheets, are renowned for their exceptional characteristics, encompassing high specific surface area and excellent conductivity, rendering them as exemplary absorbents [15,16]. Nevertheless, the pursuit of augmenting the volume fraction of nanosheets, primarily aimed at enhancing absorbent capabilities through the incorporation of additional decay elements, presents an intriguing paradox. This is mainly due to fact that dramatic increment of electrical-conductivity of composites when increasing the volume fraction of intrinsic superior electrical conductive carbon nanosheets [17–21]. Finally, it will produce a strong reflection of electromagnetic waves at the surface corresponding to a reduced absorption capacity. Therefore, lots of efforts had been devoted to the surface modification engineering to achieve desirable impedance matching and attenuation capability [22–30].

Compared to surface modification at the nanoscale, 3D structural assembly also exhibits superior absorption properties and is currently cost-effective. For example, core/shell spheres or hollow-structure sphere [31–41] could easily take full advantages of combination of core and shell absorbers, which lead to multiple relaxation processes and good impedance matching. Also, various flower-like absorbers [42–52] have been successfully fabricated by hydrothermal self-assembly strategy and showed considerable absorbing performance. This three-dimensional structure not only improves the impedance matching, produces multiple reflections and scatterings, and causes conduction loss derived from PANI, but also creates more interfacial and dipolar polarizations, which results in increased dielectric loss. Despite these advantages, the lack of tractable methodologies for precisely controlling the electromagnetic response while ensuring scalability during fabrication poses a formidable obstacle.

Carbon dots (CDs) with diameters below 10 nm have garnered significant attention due to their advantageous properties, such as low toxicity, exceptional chemical stability, abundant surface functional groups, and remarkable self-assembly capabilities under external induction [53,54]. These unique features make CDs ideal candidates for synthesizing various carbon materials with diverse morphological structures, including nanosheets materials [55] and fibers materials [56], acting as macromolecular templates and inducers. In this paper, we present a groundbreaking approach where carbon dots-derived RHC is successfully synthesized, exhibiting exceptional microwave absorption properties. Notably, the environmentally friendly aldol condensation method, performed at ambient temperature and pressure, is employed for the macroscopic generation of RHC from CDs. This approach allows for the large-scale production of RHC with remarkable cost-effectiveness and operational simplicity. This remarkable achievement lays a solid foundation for the widespread application of this superior microwave absorber.
Barium carbonate (BaCO3) was purchased from Taishan Chemical Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid and absolute ethyl alcohol were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd.

The preparation of kCDs (the kilogram-scale synthesis of carbon dots) was referred to our previous work [57]. Barium carbonate (2.0 g) and kCDs (0.4 g) were mixed uniformly and heat to specified temperature (400, 600, 700 and 800 °C) for 2 h at a rising rate of 10 °C min−1 in an argon atmosphere. The calcined product was washed several times with 1 M HCl solution and deionized water to completely remove BaCO3, which was used as a template. Finally, porous algal carbon nanosheets (RHC-400, RHC-600, RHC-700 and RHC-800) were dried in a vacuum drying oven at 80 °C.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100F) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) were used to observe the microscopic morphology of materials. The structure and morphology of ringent hollow carbon were obtained with a scanning electron microscopy (SEM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed on a Rigaku Ultimate Iv diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded by Thermo Scientific EscaLab 250Xi. Other physical properties of the samples were tested by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET, ASAP2460).

For the characterization of dielectric responses ranging from 2 GHz to 18 GHz, donut-shaped samples with 7.0 mm outer diameter and 3.0 mm inner diameter were prepared by mixing as-prepared RHCs (RHC-400, RHC-600, RHC-700 and RHC-800) with molten paraffin respectively at a certain weight loading of 50 %. The electromagnetic (EM) parameters (relative permittivity
ε r

) of samples were determined through coaxial line method with a vector network analyzer (Agilent N5230A). For accuracy of measurement, the system is carefully calibrated with Short-Open-Load-Through (SOLT) approach. Based on theory of transmission lines, the reflection loss (RL) of a metal-backed absorber with

could be expressed as:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism and microstructures of RHC
ce:section.ce:para: As reported in our previous research [57], CDs with rich functional groups typically exhibit sizes ranging from 2 to 3 nm and can spontaneously assemble into diverse carbon material structures when induced by different inorganic salts. In this study, we uniformly coat zero-dimensional CDs onto the surface of BaCO3, which serve as a stable template for subsequent reactions. Upon addition of 1 M HCl solution to the calcined products, abundant CO2 is released, resulting in the formation of ringent hollow structures with distinctive cross-sections. The mechanism of formation for the ringent hollow carbon is presented in Scheme 1 and further details are provided in the experimental section.

Fig. S1 displays images of CDs, RHC-400, RHC-600, RHC-700, and RHC-800, while the morphology and structure of RHC-400, RHC-600, RHC-700, and RHC-800 are presented in Fig. 1 , along with those of BaCO3 in Fig. S2, using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). With increasing temperature, the color of samples changes from brown to black. SEM images in Fig. 1a1–d1 reveal that all the samples were extensively cross-linked. Moreover, CDs gradually transforms into carbon nanosheets with progressively decreasing thickness, as shown in Fig. 1a2–d2, with prominent hemispherical curved layers visible along the edges of carbon nanosheets, particularly in RHC-700. TEM images in Fig. 1c2 clearly reveal the porous and hollow structures of RHC-700. Furthermore, Fig. 1d1, d2 suggest that the carbon layer of RHC-800 is so thin that the surface ruptures easily under the impact of CO2, leading to a decrease in the semi-spherical porous structure. As the temperature increases, graphite striations become increasingly evident, as shown in Fig. 1a4–d4. Detailed microstructures of CDs, RHC-400, RHC-600, RHC-700, and RHC-800 are provided in Fig. S2.

The structures of as-obtained samples are characterized by X-ray diffraction. XRD patterns show two broad peaks near 25° and 44° in Fig. 2 a, corresponding to the (002) and (100) lattice plane respectively, which means that RHC possess a low degree of crystallinity [58].

The specific surface area and pore distribution of RHC are analyzed using N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms [59]. Fig. 2b, 2c illustrate a significant presence of micropores with a diameter of 0.6 nm in RHC-700. Furthermore, it is observed that the total number of micropores (less than 2 nm) in RHC-700 is the highest among all the as-prepared materials. In the large pores region (more than 50 nm), a peak is detected in the 90 nm to 150 nm range for RHC-700, which corresponds well with the abundant semi-spherical ringent structure. The BET surface areas of RHC-400, RHC-600, RHC-700, and RHC-800 are determined to be 245.7, 476.4, 548.7, and 502.9 m2 g−1, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2d. Notably, the value for RHC-700 is the highest, which is consistent with the result of the plentiful semi-spherical hollow structure depicted in Fig. 1c1.

The survey spectra depicted in Fig. 2e indicate that RHC contains the three elements of C, H, and O. High-resolution C 1s core level XPS spectra for RHC are shown in Fig. 2f–i, which are composed of CC/CC (284.90 eV), CO (286.54 eV), and OCO (290.36 eV). Additionally, RHC-700 and RHC-800 contain CO (287.52 eV) [60]. Notably, it should be mentioned that all oxygen-containing functional groups in RHC are derived from CDs.

It is well recognized that the microwave absorption properties of most microwave absorbers are frequency dependent, and this dependence constrains the values that the real and imaginary parts of the relative permeability and relative permeability can take. As illustrated in Fig. 3 , both
ε ′
and
ε ″
increase with heat-treated temperature of RHC, especially for the RHC-700. With the increase of pyrolysis temperatures, the relative dielectric constant of RHC increases from 2.3 to 8.0 at frequency of 2 GHz (marked as black data). The same increased regularity of imaginary part of dielectric constant could be observed due to the enhanced conductivity of RHC. In addition, the
ε ′
is inclined to decrease dramatically with frequency, while
ε ″
increasing especially for RHC-700 and RHC-800. As a consequence, the loss tangent
tan δ
(i.e.,
ε ″
ε ′
) is supposed to increase with frequency, giving rise to the enhanced microwave attenuation capability. It should be noted that the relative permeability remains around 1 due to the non-magnetic propertiy of carbon materials. From this point of view, it is reasonable to assume that dielectric loss would play a dominate role in microwave energy attenuation.

As expected, the reflection loss (RL), which illustrated in Fig. 3e–h, exhibits pronounced for RHC loaded composites. Specifically, for the RHC-700 loaded sample, a strong absorption peak in the reflection loss curve is observed, reaching an optimal absorption value of −67 dB. Besides, the equivalent absorbing band for RL < −10 dB falls into frequency range higher than 4.5 GHz which covering the whole C, X and Ku band. Fortunately, a further increase in the heat treatment temperature is demonstrated to dramatically broaden the equivalent absorption band while maintaining the superior absorption strength. Another important feature to note is that, as illustrated in Fig. 3g, multiple absorption peaks with reflection loss better than −60 dB can be achieved, implying that more than 99.9999 % of the incident microwave energy can be attenuated. More importantly, the thicknesses corresponding to these absorption peaks are constrained to be smaller than 3.5 mm. This superior absorption performance makes the RHC a fascinating candidate for a microwave absorber. However, a further increase in temperature does not seem to contribute to the enhancement of microwave absorption. Note that a further enhancement of the complex dielectric permittivity is also observed. This undesirable degradation of absorption can be mainly attributed to the worse microwave impedance matching between the air and the absorber. From this point of view, moderate values of complex dielectric permittivity and strong fluctuation relaxation are quite essential for high performance absorption.

From the normalized input impedance curve, the input impedance of RHC-400 and RHC-600 samples is close to 1 only when the thickness is less than 1 mm, and the input impedance value is significantly greater than 1 in most other cases, which also leads to the unsatisfactory reflection loss curve. In contrast, the normalized input impedance of RHC-700 and RHC-800 samples can reach the ideal value 1 in the extremely wide range of thickness from 0.1 mm to 5 mm, which also corresponds to excellent wave absorption performance in a wide range of thickness, providing greater freedom of choice for coating construction applications.

For further evaluation of microwave absorbing performance of RHC, radar cross section (RCS) of typical-selected RHC-700 coated square metal plate have been evaluated through ANSYS Electronics Desktop simulation. The models have a lesser appearance than the perfect electric conductor (PEC) model and other samples model, indicating that RHC-700 specimen coating has the minimum RCS value. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the 3D intensity map of the reflected signal clearly depends on the azimuth and pitch angles. For clarity, the RCS curves for the RHC-700 coated square metal plate at the H-plane are illustrated in Fig. 4b, together with the metal plane marked in blue. Clearly, compressed RCS can be observed for all incident angle ranges after coating with RHC-700 on a metallic plate. With perpendicular incidence, a relative compression ratio of 56.7 percent is achievable.

Experimental reflection loss evaluations and RCS predictions have demonstrated that the superior microwave absorption performance of RHC enabled composites mainly originates from intrinsic attenuation within the assembly structure. To put this in perspective, the volume loss distribution of the RHC has been simulated with the help of finite element simulations to explore the mechanism of microwave absorption. According to the SEM investigations, RHCs (i.e., RHC-400, RHC-600, RHC-700 and RHC-800) could be rationally equivalent to solid carbon sphere (see Fig. 5 a2), hollow carbon sphere (see Fig. 5b2), partially ringent hollow carbon (see Fig. 5c2) and fracture hollow carbon (see Fig. 5d2), respectively. The average diameter of RHC is 0.67 um with wall thickness of 0.01um with help of SEM and TEM investigation (See in Fig. 1(c1–c4). The finite element method (FEM) is adopted to implement the numerical simulation. Three-dimensional simulation model of the RHC is illustrated as follow. Two pairs of master–slave boundary conditions are employed to simulate periodic structure along E and H axes, respectively. A floquet port is designated on the top boundary to generate the incident waves along the incident wave, irradiating onto the upper surface and lower surface of the proposed model.

As clearly shown in Fig. 5, the high-volume loss space is mainly confined near the top of the spheroid and the ridge walls of the annular hollow carbon. While for RHC-700, most of the incident wave is either scattered in other directions or decays inside the hollow carbon sphere, giving rise to very little normal reflection. This is consistent with the results shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. 5a4–d4 concerning the scattering distribution of the reflection loss. More specifically, maximum volume loss density as well as relative reflection coefficient (fixing reflection coefficient of RHC-400 equals to 1) could be extracted based on Fig. 5. As clearly shown in Fig. 6 , the highest volume loss densities, as well as the lowest relative reflection coefficients, are observed in RHC-700. As a result, it is clear to conclude that the unique structure of partially annular hollow carbon is seen as an effective way to lower normal reflected waves and promote energy decay.

To further explore the mechanism of microwave loss of RHCs particles, the scattering field distribution of RHCs particles at different opening orientations was simulated and calculated, which is illustrated in Fig. 7 . Here, the influence of different incident angles on the dissipation of the incident electromagnetic wave is investigated in detail. That is, the incident waves are all top-down, and the opening direction of the sphere changes from upward to downward. Simulations show that when the angle between the opening direction and the normal direction is acute, the amplitude of the electric field is more significant than for other large angles, and the intensity of the scattered field is almost half of that for other angles. This means that the reflection of the RHCs particles on the electromagnetic wave is significantly lower than the reflection at other angles, which is very favorable for improving the absorption performance. From the structural point of view, such an opening structure constitutes a wave-trapping structure, which can guide electromagnetic waves to scatter several times inside the hollow sphere. Moreover, when the angle of the opening is acute, almost most of the incident electromagnetic wave can be captured, and the energy of the electromagnetic wave will be dissipated in the carbon RHCs, thus exhibiting excellent absorption properties.

Comparing our results with the previously reported microsphere absorbers (Table 1 ), the absorption bands cover C, X and Ku bands completely and there is perfect wave absorption in a rather thin thickness range (less than 3.5 mm). In addition, the ringent hollow carbon microspheres in this work are less dense and can be prepared in large quantities.

In this study, we have successfully implemented porous algal carbon nanosheets with microwave absorption functions in a ring-shaped hollow structure using discs at various calcination temperatures. We discovered that the ringent hollow carbon manufactured at 700 °C showed remarkable microwave absorption properties after comparing the samples. Optimal absorption values of −67 dB and superior absorption bandwidths covering the C, X and Ku bands have been achieved. Radar cross section (RCS) of typical RHC-700 coated square metal plate have been evaluated to further confirm superior microwave absorbing performance. A relative compression ratio of 56.7 % can be achieved in the perpendicular incidence case compared to the metallic slab. Further, the volume loss distribution of RHCs has been simulated with the help of finite element simulations to explore the mechanism of microwave absorption. Simulations have found that most of the incident wave is either scattered in other directions or decays inside the hollow carbon sphere, giving rise to very little normal reflection. It was concluded that the unique structure of partially annular hollow carbon is considered as an effective way to lower normal reflected waves and promote energy decay.

Yinger Xiang: Investigation, Resources, Writing - original draft. Jinfei Zheng: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Heng Luo: Data curation, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Hira Rehman: Validation, Resources. Guoqiang Zou: Data curation, Formal analysis. Jinchao Tong: Supervision. Hongshuai Hou: Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Xiaobo Ji: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51802352), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (Grant No. 2021JJ40789).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.158353.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
